extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a becomes true only via r1, whose condition is the conjunction of all six comorbidity flags including prior_gi_bleed. rec_drug_b becomes true only via r3, whose condition is prior_gi_bleed alone. Whenever r1's condition is satisfied, prior_gi_bleed is necessarily true, so r3's condition is also satisfied. No rule competes for rec_drug_a or rec_drug_b (r2 only sets rec_drug_c), so priority/order conflict resolution cannot suppress either output. Therefore any input that fires r1 also fires r3, producing rec_drug_a = true and rec_drug_b = true simultaneously, violating the mutual-exclusion property. Concrete counterexample: set prior_gi_bleed, asthma, ckd_dialysis, qt_prolongation, low_ejection_fraction, bradycardia all true (diabetes irrelevant, set false). Then r1 -> rec_drug_a = true and r3 -> rec_drug_b = true, both jointly true.
